titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Cecil Herbert Driver Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Manuscripts and Archives Staff

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday August 20, 2020 at 09:47
Finding aid revision description not supplied.

did.abstract: Correspondence, research materials, clippings, photographs, and reviews of Driver's biography, Tory Radical: The Life of Richard Oastler , published in 1946. Most significant in the papers is a series of eight letters written in 1832 by Oastler to Thomas Daniels, the secretary of the Manchester Short-Time Committee, a letter from Oastler's wife, Mary, and another from Michael Thomas Sadler, the author of the Ten-Hour Bill, to Oastler. Driver's correspondents include Roland H. Bainton, Lewis P. Curtis, Edgar Johnson, Broadus Mitchell, Clinton L. Rossiter, and Hume Wrong.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Cecil Herbert Driver Papers (MS 782). Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Transferred from the Department of Political Science, 1960 and gift of Mrs. Charles M. Lyman, 1976.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The materials in this collection relate to Cecil Deiver's biography, Tory Radical: The Life of Richard Oastler , published in 1946. There are approximately thirty-five letters, mostly comments and congratulations from people who had read the book, although a few of the letters date from before its publication and deal with research interests and other professional matters. An alphabetical list of correspondents can be found on page 4. Also in the collection are notebooks kept by Driver on Karl Marx and on concepts and theories of the nature of the universe, and a typed copy of Leopold Boissier's Droit constitutionnel comparè . An alphabetical list of correspondents can be found on page 4.

The remainder of the collection consists of notebooks and research materials for Tory Radical , which pertain largely to Richard Oastler, political activist and organizer in the 1830s of the Ten-Hour Movement, a campaign of workingmen for legislation limiting child labor to ten hours per day. Among Driver's research materials are photographs, printed matter about Oastler, and photostatic copies of several letters from Oastler, of posters and handbills, and of other miscellaneous printed items. Of particular interest are ten original letters (folder 15) addressed to Thomas Daniels, the secretary of the Manchester Short-Time Committee and later chairman of the Central Operatives Committee, two organizations devoted to the Ten-Hour Movement. Eight of the letters are from 0astler, who writes to encourage Daniels' efforts and to plan activities and publicity. The ninth letter is from Oastler's wife, Mary, and the last is from Michael Thomas Sadler, the author of the Ten-Hour Bill, who writes concerning a petition by the people of Manchester to the electors of Leeds. The final items in the collection are the dustjacket of Tory Radical , a copy of Driver's agreement with Oxford University Press, and clippings and reviews of the book.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright status for collection materials is unknown, though much of the material in this collection is likely in the public domain. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

subject.part: Laboring and laboring classes -- Great Britain

did.unittitle: Notebook on concepts and theories of the nature of the universe
Richard Oastler, the Factory King in his study at Fixby Hall, Huddersfield
Richard Oastler at the age of forty-eight
Oastler in old age, from a photograph taken shortly before his death (3)

Statue of Richard Oastler now in Rawson Square, Bradford. Unveiled by Lord Shaftesbury, 1869. The reproduction is taken from the Illustrated London News which erroneously locates the statue at Halifax (3)
Another view of the above statue
Fixby Hall, near Huddersfield as it was during Oastler's stewardship
Henry Hetherington, leader of the agitation against taxes on newspaper, and editor of "The Poor Man's Guardian" (2)
The Oastler National Testimonial Medal (front and back)
ALS, Richard Oastler to Lord Viscount Milton (photostat), 3 pages
ALS, Richard Oastler to Lord Viscount Milton (photostat), 4 pages
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 3 pages
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 3 pages, address on verso
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 3 pages, address on verso
ALS, Mary Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 1 page, address on verso
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 4 pages, address on last page
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 4 pages, address on last page
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 1 page, address on verso
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 2 pages, address on verso
ALS, Richard Oastler to Thomas Daniels, 2 pages, address on verso
ALS, Michael Thomas Sadler to Thomas Daniels, 2 pages, address on verso
The Cloth Hall, the White Hart Inn & Market Street
Old Market Place & the George Inn, Huddersfield
Michael Thomas Sadler, author of "The Law of Population"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The poster put out by Oastler to announce the County Meeting of April 24, 1832. This was distributed throughout the Riding.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: A poster put out by the Huddersfield Short Time Committee to explain the plans for their area. Similar posters were put out by the other local committees.

did.unittitle: The Leeds Electoral Contest: October to December (2)

odd.head: General
odd.p: One of the many appeals on behalf of Michael Sadler sent sent to Leeds by the operatives of other manufacturing districts.

odd.head: General
odd.p: One of the innumberable posters put forth by the millowners in opposition to Ashley's Ten Hours Bill. This one appeared in Manchester while petitions from employers were being sent to the House of Commons appealing for a second investigation of factory conditions. The Sadler Report had been published ten weeks earlier.

odd.head: General
odd.p: A poster put out by the Lancashire Central Short Time Committee in reply to the one reproduced above. Similar posters were distributed by the Yorkshire Central Committee, as well as pamphlets developing the same arguments.

did.unittitle: General Summons - To the West Riding meeting on Wibsey Low Moor, July 1 (2)

odd.head: General
odd.p: Put out by the Huddersfield Short Time Committee.

did.unittitle: Directions for the West Riding Demonstration of July 1 (2)

odd.head: General
odd.p: Put out by the Central Committee, Bradford.

did.unittitle: The Protest Against the Royal Commission (2)

odd.head: General
odd.p: One of the many posters put out by the West Riding Central Short Time Committee calling for a meeting of protest against the Royal Commission. Similar posters were used by the local Committees at Leeds, Huddersield, Dewsbury & elsewhere.

odd.head: General
odd.p: Another poster distributed throughout the West Riding by the local Short Time Committees.

did.unittitle: Mr. Oastler's Farewell to Fixby Hall
Richard Oastler's reply to the "Factory Masters" in Cragg Dale (2)
Poster announcing the appearance of Richard Oastler & G.S. Bull (2)
Reply to R. Oastler by the "Factory Masters" in Cragg-Valley (2)

title.part: Report of the Proceedings of the Public Meeting on the Ten Hours Act, on the 25th of July, 1849

did.unittitle: Biographical Sketch of Richard Oastler from the Illustrated London News
Dustjacket for Tory Radical: The Life of Richard Oastler by Cecil Driver
Agreement between Cecil Driver and Oxford University Press, New York, Inc. for the publication of Tory Radical: The Life of Richard Oastler
Clippings & reviews of Tory Radical: The Life of Richard Oastler
Posters & handbills (one positive & one negative photostat of each)
Directions for the West Riding Demonstration
The Protest Against the Royal Commission
Protest Against the Defeat of the Ten Hours Clause
Richard Oastler's reply to the "Factory Masters"
Poster announcing the appearance of Richard Oastler & G.S. Bull
Reply to R. Oastler by the "Factory Masters" (2 positives & 1 negative photostat)
Portrait of the Late Richard Oastler, from "The Illustrated News of the World" (1 positive & 2 negatives)
Instructions to the Short Time Committees, Manchester (1 negative)
Memorandum of 22 questions dealing with factory working conditions (1 negative) 3 pages
Representation of Leeds [from the "Leeds Intelligencer" (1 negative) 2 pages
Richard Oastler's participation in the proceedings at Leeds (1 negative) 6 pages
